scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 5 anti-patterns identified by name: shared database (Order/Courier), synchronous Notification fan-in, god endpoint (/everything), REST-based background jobs, direct cross-service DB reads (Reporting→Order). Remediation is clearly prioritized by risk severity across 5 phases, not treated equally. Shared database and direct DB reads identified as highest risk (critical/high). REST-based jobs identified as fragile with specific risks (timeouts, accidental triggering). Trade-offs for shared database split are thorough (loss of ACID joins, eventual consistency, migration effort). Incremental strangler fig approach used throughout — explicitly warns against big-bang rewrite multiple times.

observations.text: All 5 anti-patterns clearly identified with specific explanations of why each is harmful
Phased remediation plan with clear sequencing rationale and timeline estimates
Excellent trade-off analysis for each remediation with alternatives considered
Strangler fig pattern recommended consistently — never suggests big-bang migration
Pitfalls section adds practical value beyond the rubric requirements (e.g., vulnerability scanner triggering refund jobs)
Domain model with bounded contexts and context map adds architectural depth

Remediation priority order slightly deviates from rubric: ranks Notification async (Phase 2) above Reporting DB reads (Phase 3), whereas rubric groups shared DB and direct DB reads together as highest risk. However, the reasoning is defensible given the 5-service blast radius of Notification.
